channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for www.gamedeveloper.com

item.title: CWA Canada president says Saudi buyout of EA should trigger 'virtually every alarm bell'
item.content:encoded:
Chris Kerr,

Senior Editor, News,GameDeveloper.com

May 6, 2026

4 Min Read

Labor union CWA Canada has called on the Canadian government to scrutinize the proposed $55 billion buyout of Electronic Arts over perceived national security risks. 

The Battlefield and EA Sports FC developer is being acquired by a consortium that includes Saudi Arabia's state-owned investment vehicle, Public Investment Fund (PIF), and private investment firms Silver Lake and Affinity Partners—the latter of which was established by Jared Kushner, the son-in-law of U.S. president Donald Trump. 

EA currently operates multiple studios across Canada in locations such as Quebec, Vancouver, Toronto, and Victoria.

In a letter sent to Canadian minister of industry Mélanie Joly on May 5, 2026, CWA Canada president Carmel Smyth said the union has "serious concerns" about the national security implications of the proposed takeover. 

"We are deeply concerned that a foreign sovereign wealth fund tasked with implementing the Saudi Arabian government's strategy to 'become the global hub for gaming and esports' plans to acquire one of the most successful North American video game publishers, with extensive operations in Canada, reaching hundreds of millions of players globally," reads the letter, shared with Game Developer via email. 

Related:Xbox appoints new internal leaders and ditches Copilot AI

"[...] In addition to the sheer size and strategy behind this deal, it is also highly relevant that, because the deal would put EA under the near-absolute control of PIF, not only would EA be closely economically integrated with the Saudi Arabian government, but control would effectively be solely in the hands of Crown Prince Mohammed Bin Salman, the unelected monarch of a country with, at best, a very different approach from Canada to personal autonomy and privacy and at worst a highly troubling record of human rights abuses including surveillance and misuse of information."

Advocacy groups such as Amnesty International and Human Rights Watch have strongly criticized Saudi Arabia's human rights record under the leadership of bin Salman, who they claim has suppressed freedom of speech by outlawing protests, criminalising LGBTQ+ relationships and gender expression, and discriminating against women.

Bin Salman has also been linked to the 2018 murder of Washington Post journalist Jamal Khashoggi, and faced questions about his alleged involvement in the killing during a White House press conference in November 2025.

"The structure of the proposed acquisition puts the thousands of Canadian EA workers at increased risk"

In the letter, Smyth suggested the Saudi Arabian government "under the singular authority of Crown Prince Salman" is unlikely to operate EA in a way that is consistent with Canadian rights and values, and said the deal will allow the Saudi regime to collect and leverage the sensitive personal data of millions of Canadians.

Related:Valve to restock Steam Controller after it sells out in a day

According to Smyth, that alone should be ringing "virtually every alarm bell regarding potential national security risks."

"Saudi Arabia could utilize its access to Canadians' personal information for purposes of mass surveillance, censorship, or propaganda, and we are highly skeptical Saudi Arabia would engage in these activities in a manner that is consistent with Canadian values," she continued. 

"Finally, because EA is heavily invested in developing artificial intelligence (AI), the transaction could also result in the transfer of sensitive technology to a foreign state, also an acute national security concern under the ICA." 

The 'ICA' refers to the Investment Canada Act, a Canadian federal law Smyth explained is designed to balance a positive investment climate while safeguarding Canada from "foreign actors that attempt to gain ownership or control of sensitive Canadian goods, technology, infrastructure, or personal data" for nefarious reasons.

Smyth claimed the EA buyout fits that profile—but she has other concerns over how the deal might impact the livelihoods of workers at the global publisher. 

Related:Metacore restructuring to the tune of 'up to 160' redundancies

"Workers in the video game industry have experienced significant layoffs in recent years, and the structure of the proposed acquisition puts the thousands of Canadian EA workers at increased risk," she continued. "The deal is being financed with US$20 billion in debt; as a result, some analysts suggest layoffs are one of the key ways the company will seek to cut costs in order to service its massive debt."

EA previously suggested the buyout won't result in "immediate" job cuts. It has since cut a number of roles across Battlefield Studios. The company also pledged to foster a culture of 'creativity, pioneering, passion, determination, learning, and teamwork' under its new owners. 

Even so, Smyth fears the Saudi Arabian state—which will own 93.4 percent of EA if the deal is approved—might begin using EA titles as vehicles for "mass surveillance and state-sponsored propaganda." As a result, she has urged the Canadian government to "blow the whistle" on the deal. 

This isn't the first time the buyout has been called into question. Last year, a group of unionized EA employees argued the proposed acquisition will likely harm workers and called on regulators in the United States to place it under the microscope. 

U.S. senators Richard Blumenthal and Elizabeth Warren also expressed concerns about the national security risks posed by the take-private. In January, another group of U.S. lawmakers said the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) must review the acquisition over fears it could incentive layoffs, offshoring, studio closures, and other cost-cutting measures.

About the Author

Senior Editor, News, GameDeveloper.com

Game Developer news editor Chris Kerr is an award-winning reporter with over a decade of experience in the game industry. His byline has appeared in notable print and digital publications including Edge, Stuff, Wireframe, International Business Times, and PocketGamer.biz. Throughout his career, Chris has covered major industry events including GDC, PAX Australia, Gamescom, Paris Games Week, and Develop Brighton. 



https://www.gamedeveloper.com/business/union-president-says-saudi-buyout-of-ea-should-trigger-virtually-every-alarm-bell-
